cdp.name: CDP
cdp.gloss: Customer Data Platform — the unified store of who your customers are.

perso.name: Personalization (1:1)
perso.gloss: Tailoring the message to the individual, not the segment.

genai.name: Generative AI
genai.gloss: Models that produce content — copy, images, replies — from a prompt.

agentic.name: Agentic AI
agentic.gloss: Systems that don't just generate, they decide and act toward a goal.

decis.name: Agentic decisioning
decis.gloss: An agent choosing what to say, when, how often and on which channel — per person.

bandit.name: Bandits / RL
bandit.gloss: The maths that lets a system learn which choice wins by trying and measuring.

cep.name: CEP
cep.gloss: Customer Engagement Platform — where this messaging actually runs.

geo.name: GEO
geo.gloss: Generative Engine Optimization — being visible inside AI answers.

govern.name: Governance
govern.gloss: The controls that make automated decisions safe, explainable and legible.

stories.head: 5 questions to ask AI vendors before you buy anything
stories.plain: A practical buying guide for marketers evaluating AI tools, covering business-value proof, data policies, and implementation realities. This matters because most buyers are still being sold on demos and hype; a clear evaluation checklist directly changes how AI gets procured and what governance questions get asked before contracts are signed.

stories.head: Omneky Launches Public API and MCP Server for Autonomous Ad Creative Generation
stories.plain: Omneky has opened its AI ad-creative engine to outside developers and AI agents via a public API and Model Context Protocol server, meaning any platform or agentic workflow can now generate and deploy ad creatives programmatically. This shifts autonomous creative production from a walled product into open infrastructure other tools can plug into.

stories.head: When AI Answers the Call: Where Autonomous AI Belongs in the Contact Center
stories.plain: Webex's Chang Chang argues that using AI purely for call deflection misses the point — the real measure is whether customers were actually helped. The piece reframes how contact-center AI should be governed and deployed, pushing teams to design for resolution quality rather than volume reduction, which shifts how CEPs are evaluated.

stories.head: Docket CEO on Turning Every Site Visit into a Real Qualifying Conversation via AI
stories.plain: Docket's platform uses agentic conversational AI to qualify visitor intent, book meetings, and push results directly into a CRM in real time — replacing static forms and chat bots. It illustrates how decisioning agents are being embedded at the top of the funnel, changing how marketing pipelines are built and measured.

stories.head: Transactional, Triggered, and Promotional Emails: What's the Difference?
stories.plain: A foundational explainer distinguishing the three core email types by purpose, legal rules, and revenue impact. Useful for new MarTech readers because misclassifying triggered versus promotional sends creates compliance risk and deliverability problems — a practical governance issue that surfaces early when teams start automating customer engagement at scale.

stories.head: 5 questions to ask AI vendors before you buy anything
stories.plain: A practical buying guide that cuts through AI hype by telling marketers exactly what to probe before signing a contract — covering business value proof, data policies, real customer results, and implementation reality. Anyone evaluating AI tools right now needs this checklist to avoid costly mistakes and hold vendors accountable.

stories.head: AI adoption hits 90% as CX deployment paths diverge
stories.plain: New research shows AI use in customer experience is nearly universal, but organizations are splitting sharply on architecture choices, governance models, and how to build customer trust while proving ROI. This divergence will determine which companies pull ahead — making it a key strategic signal for anyone buying or building CX AI.

stories.head: Omneky Launches Public API and MCP Server for Autonomous Ad Creative Generation
stories.plain: Omneky opened its AI creative engine to external developers and AI agents via a public API and Model Context Protocol server, meaning any platform or agent can now autonomously generate and deploy ad creative. This shifts generative ad production from a closed tool into an interoperable infrastructure layer — a significant build-vs-buy moment.

stories.head: Context, not the model, now decides whether your AI stack pays off
stories.plain: Emerging research argues the underlying AI model is now a commodity — what separates winning stacks from wasteful ones is the quality of data, real-time signals, and machine-readable brand context fed to agents. This reframes where marketers should invest: away from model access and toward data infrastructure and brand context management.

stories.head: The CMS is becoming the AI operating system for brands
stories.plain: A strategic analysis arguing that choosing a CMS now means choosing your AI discovery, personalization, and governance foundation — not just a publishing tool. As AI agents and generative search pull content, the CMS layer controls what gets surfaced and how brand assets are structured for machines, making it a core infrastructure decision.

month.skicker: 30-day synthesis · the shape underneath the feed

themes.head: Autonomous AI Agents Move from Pilot to Pipeline
body: AI agents are no longer theoretical — they are being wired directly into marketing pipelines. Omneky's public API and MCP server let autonomous ad-creative generation run inside any platform or agent, while Webex and Hermes Agent Desktop show the same pattern arriving in contact centers and martech stacks.

The decision shift for marketers is where to grant agents authority, not whether to try them. Deployment paths are diverging fast: some teams give agents narrow, supervised tasks; others are handing over full workflow loops. Getting the boundary wrong in either direction is now a material business risk.
ev: Agile Brand · Webex contact center AI

themes.head: The CMS Becomes an AI Operating System
body: Content management platforms are quietly being re-architected as the central intelligence layer for brand output. Rather than storing and publishing pages, they are becoming the environment where context, data, and AI models combine to generate, personalize, and distribute content at scale — a fundamental replatforming of the marketing stack.

This matters to decision-makers because budget and vendor choices made around the CMS today will constrain AI capability for years. Alongside this, the martech futurist piece on context costs underlines that the richness and structure of the data fed into that system now directly determines both what AI can do and what it will charge you.

themes.head: AI Search & Trust Rewrite Visibility Rules
body: As AI-powered search surfaces answers instead of links, traditional SEO ranking factors are losing primacy. Trust signals — citations, authoritative sourcing, brand credibility — are becoming the primary lever for whether a brand appears in AI-generated answers at all. The funnel entry point has moved upstream of the click.

For marketers this reframes content investment: producing volume is less valuable than producing verifiable, citable, structured content that AI systems will confidently surface. The 'marketing funnel is lying' argument reinforces this — models of awareness built on search-click data are increasingly blind to how buyers actually discover and evaluate brands in an AI-mediated world.
ev: Agile Brand · Why your funnel is lying

themes.head: AI Vendor Due Diligence Becomes a Core Competency
body: With AI adoption now above 90% in CX functions, the buying question has shifted from 'should we adopt?' to 'how do we evaluate what we're buying?' A checklist of five vendor questions — covering data use, model provenance, auditability, integration depth, and pricing structure — signals that procurement rigor is catching up to the hype cycle.

The risk of skipping due diligence is compounding: vendor lock-in, hidden context costs, and model opacity can undermine the very efficiency gains AI promises. The same pressure is visible in 'vibe coding' culture — scaling AI-assisted development without governance frameworks creates technical and legal debt faster than teams can manage.

themes.head: AI Is Creating a Marketing Talent & Skills Gap
body: If AI executes the repetitive, judgment-building tasks that used to train junior marketers, the industry faces a structural pipeline problem: who develops the contextual instincts needed to supervise, correct, and direct AI systems? This is not a soft HR concern — it directly affects output quality, brand safety, and the long-term ability to use AI strategically.

The wave of senior leadership appointments seen at Qualtrics and Klaviyo this month reflects organizations trying to buy their way into AI-era capability at the top, but that does not solve the mid-level vacuum. Teams that invest deliberately in human skill development alongside AI tooling will have a durable competitive edge over those that treat AI purely as a headcount substitute.

month.takeaway: Across every layer of the stack, the month's news converges on a single pressure: AI is forcing marketers to make irreversible architectural, talent, and trust decisions faster than most governance frameworks can keep up.
